g.text: idx=00 | char=' " ' | U+0022 | token boundary candidate
idx=07 | char='/' | U+002F | AMBIGUOUS — depends on mode
idx=08 | char='/' | U+002F | AMBIGUOUS — depends on mode
NORMAL | default mode — full token dispatch active
IN_STRING | inside string literal — most chars are content
IN_LINE_CMT | inside // comment — discard until newline
KEY RULE: mode is determined by scanner, not by character alone
Step 1: Scanner Sees '"' in NORMAL Mode → Enter IN_STRING
Step 2: IN_STRING Mode — Characters Are Content, Not Operators
idx=15 | '"' | IN_STRING → CLOSING QUOTE → emit STRING
Character | NORMAL mode meaning | IN_STRING mode meaning
'/' | division OR comment start | literal content char
'"' | start string literal | END string literal
RULE: advance() + peek() determine char meaning, NOT char value alone
Preprocessing has NO state: it cannot know
whether '//' appears inside a string or not.
'val // note' — the '//' is a real comment
Both look identical to a context-free stripper.
if ch == '"': # ONLY this closes the string

text.tspan: # '//' at idx 7-8: just two content chars, ignored
# '//' at idx 7-8: just two content chars, ignored

g.text: lexeme includes both quote chars and all 16 content chars
The scanner is a DFA. Each mode = a DFA state.
In NORMAL mode: '/' can start a comment
In IN_STRING mode: '/' is always literal content
Same byte (0x2F), different state → different action
This context-sensitivity is impossible in preprocessing
